# Statistical Limitations of the Accuracy-Delta Analysis

**Owner:** Abdelrahman (Metrics & Analysis)
**Source data:** `outputs/accuracy_delta_validated.csv`
**Generated by:** `scripts/validate_accuracy_delta.py`

> ⚠️ **Note on the numbers in this document.** This version was written
> against the pipeline's current output, which at the time of writing
> reflects placeholder/test inference data, not the final ImageNet-truck
> run. The specific class names, counts, and deltas below will change once
> Ahmed's real dataset and Catherine's real `inference_*.csv` files are in
> place. **Re-run `scripts/accuracy_delta.py` then
> `scripts/validate_accuracy_delta.py` on the final data and update the
> numbers in this file before final submission.** The methodology and
> conclusions about *how* to read the CI columns remain valid regardless.

---

## 1. Why this matters

`accuracy_delta.csv` reports a per-class accuracy drop between clean images
and a modified version (logo blurred / replaced / cropped). A drop like
"beer_truck accuracy fell 44% under blur" sounds like strong evidence of
Clever-Hans behaviour — but accuracy is a proportion estimated from a
*finite sample* of test images. With only 10–20 images per class, a single
misclassification swings the reported accuracy by 5–10 percentage points.
A "20% delta" computed from 10 images per side is not the same kind of
evidence as a "20% delta" computed from 100 images per side, even though
both show up as the same number in the raw CSV.

To make this distinction visible, `validate_accuracy_delta.py` attaches a
**95% Wilson confidence interval** to both the clean and modified accuracy
for every class/method row, and derives two flags:

- **`low_n_flag`** — `True` if either the clean or modified sample for that
  class has fewer than **30** images. Below this, the CI is generally too
  wide to draw a confident conclusion either way.
- **`delta_significant`** — `True` only if `low_n_flag` is `False` **and**
  the two 95% confidence intervals (clean vs. modified) do not overlap.
  Non-overlapping CIs are a reasonable (if informal) signal that the
  difference reflects a real effect rather than sampling noise.

### Why Wilson intervals specifically

The textbook "p ± 1.96·√(p(1−p)/n)" interval (the Wald interval) behaves
badly exactly in the regime we're in here: small n, and accuracy values
that often sit near 0% or 100%. Wilson's score interval corrects for this
and is the standard recommendation for small-sample proportion estimation,
so it's what the validation script uses throughout.

---

## 2. Which classes have low N

Based on the current run, the following classes have **fewer than 30
images** in at least one clean/modified comparison, and are flagged
`low_n_flag = True` for every method row they appear in:

| Class | n (clean) | n (blur) | n (replace) | n (crop) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| fire_engine | 15 | 15 | 15 | 15 |
| semi_truck | 10 | 10 | 10 | 10 |
| tank_truck | 12 | 12 | 12 | 12 |

**Implication:** any delta reported for these three classes — no matter how
large it looks in `accuracy_delta.png` — should be treated as **suggestive,
not conclusive**. For example, `fire_engine` shows a 33% accuracy drop
under blur in the raw numbers, but with only 15 images per side the 95%
CIs for clean (70–99%) and modified (36–80%) still overlap, so we cannot
rule out that this is sampling noise rather than a real reliance on the
logo. The fix is more test images per class, not a different statistical
method — 30 is a conventional floor, not a guarantee, and higher n will
narrow the intervals further.

**Action item:** if these three classes are important to the paper's
conclusions (fire_engine looks like the most likely genuine shortcut
learner), Ahmed should prioritize collecting more test images for
`fire_engine`, `semi_truck`, and `tank_truck` before the analysis is
finalized.

---

## 3. What the confidence intervals imply

Reading a row in `accuracy_delta_validated.csv`:

```
class=beer_truck, method=blur, accuracy_clean=0.88, accuracy_modified=0.44
ci_clean_low=0.762, ci_clean_high=0.944
ci_modified_low=0.312, ci_modified_high=0.577
```

This means: *if we re-ran this same evaluation on a different random
sample of beer_truck images of the same size, we'd expect the clean
accuracy to land somewhere in 76–94% about 95% of the time, and the
blurred accuracy to land in 31–58%.* Because those two ranges don't
overlap at all, it's very unlikely the ~44-point gap is just noise — this
is the strongest evidence in the dataset for a real Clever-Hans effect on
`beer_truck` under logo blurring.

Compare that to `dump_truck/blur`, where accuracy actually goes *up*
slightly under blur (delta = −6.7%) with heavily overlapping CIs — this is
consistent with no real effect, i.e. the model isn't relying on the logo
for this class.

---

## 4. Which deltas are statistically meaningful

Out of the 24 class × method rows currently in the pipeline, **3 are
flagged `delta_significant = True`** (sufficient sample size and
non-overlapping CIs):

| Class | Method | Delta | Clean 95% CI | Modified 95% CI |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| beer_truck | blur | +44.0% | 76.2–94.4% | 31.2–57.7% |
| tow_truck | blur | +53.3% | 75.8–93.1% | 22.7–45.9% |
| beer_truck | replace | +24.0% | 76.2–94.4% | 50.1–75.9% |

These are the cases where we can say with reasonable statistical
confidence that the model's accuracy genuinely drops when the logo is
altered — i.e., real evidence of shortcut learning, not sampling noise.

Everything else in the current run — including several rows with large
raw deltas, like `fire_engine/crop` at 46.7% — is **not** flagged as
significant, either because the sample is too small (`low_n_flag`) or
because the CIs overlap despite the point estimate looking large. These
should be reported as *preliminary/suggestive* findings in any write-up,
not as confirmed shortcut-learning evidence, until more data narrows the
intervals.

**Bottom line for the report:** lead with the `delta_significant = True`
rows as the dataset's clearest evidence of logo/text shortcut reliance;
present the flagged low-n classes as open questions requiring more data
rather than settled conclusions; and note that a non-significant delta
does *not* prove the model is shortcut-free — it just means this dataset,
at this sample size, can't distinguish a real effect from noise.

---

## 5. Dashboard integration

Per the Day 4 task, the dashboard (`src/dashboard/`, owned by Hassan)
should pull **Tab 1** from `outputs/accuracy_delta_validated.csv` instead
of `outputs/accuracy_delta.csv`, so end users see the CI and low-n flags
alongside the raw deltas rather than raw deltas alone. Coordinating this
change is Hassan's to make in `src/dashboard/`; this doc and the validated
CSV are the inputs he needs.
